Same root cause as before — UI's 60-sec HTTP timeout vs Charles taking ~2 minutes for 17+ rounds of work. The POST gets cancelled but **server-side respond keeps running and writes to the DB when done**. The architectural fix is what I flagged earlier: change `/api/command/message` from synchronous (waits for full reply) to async (returns immediately with a job_id, UI polls). Should be ~30 lines. I'll build it as soon as this run completes — but keeping eyes on logs first.

Honest assessment with the live data in front of me:

**What's actually working well:**
- Goal #10002 completed autonomously overnight. 57 `url_corpus` facts + 6 auto-extracted findings + 760KB Learn You a Haskell file. Real output.
- He uses `recall()` as round 0 reliably now — auto-recall + seeded facts gave him memory continuity.
- Async API: zero UI timeouts since deployed.
- Sqlite3 redirect, dedup escalation, BLOCKED headers — all firing cleanly when needed.
- Auto-poll Mac UI showing replies in 2 sec post-rebuild.

**What's still suboptimal (but non-fatal):**

1. **Recall-iteration loops.** Just watched him spend 20 rounds doing `recall("url_corpus url:1")`, `url:11`, `url:12`... with empty results. His tag schema assumption (url:N) didn't match what he actually saved overnight. Pure wasted rounds. Same-call dedup doesn't catch this because each query has a different N.

2. **Auto-recall budget too generous.** 5 facts × no char cap = 30K+ chars in the prompt for some queries. Trim to 3 facts × 1500 chars cap. Won't lose useful context.

3. **In-memory URL block-list resets on process restart.** Last night I had blocks for ResearchGate / Cloudflare / etc. After my agent restart, those evaporate. Should persist to DB so a 2-day scrape session doesn't re-hit the same dead URLs.

4. **Tool-only-turn loops invisible to intra-call guard.** When Charles emits `tool_call` with empty `content`, the loop detector doesn't track it (needs assistant text). Tool-call signatures should track too.

**The "without cutting his nuts" calibrated list:**

| Fix | Cost | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuzzy-recall nudge after 4 similar recall queries return short results** | ~30 lines | Low — just a hint after he's already wasted 4 rounds, doesn't block him |
| **Tighten auto-recall to 3 facts × 1500 char cap** | 5 lines | Trivial — current 30K is overkill |
| **Persist URL block-list to long_term_facts (tagged `blocked_url,<reason>`)** | ~40 lines | Low — pure addition, no behavior loss |
| Tool-call-signature tracking in intra-call guard | ~20 lines | Slightly higher — could false-positive on legitimate parallel tool calls; would need testing |

Then the honest answer is harder than I want to give you, but here it is:

**At your hardware + sovereignty + free constraints, you're at the model ceiling. Qwen 3.6-A3B-4bit IS the model. There is no better one that fits. No framework swap fixes that.**

The deeper reason "Are you awake" fails: A3B = "3B active params per forward pass" via MoE routing. That 35B total is mostly storage capacity — for any single reasoning step, Charles is reasoning with 3B-model intelligence. That's roughly the size of a phone-class LLM. It's why he over-fits on context, takes user words too literally, gets stuck in loops. Bigger is needed for those behaviors but bigger doesn't fit.

So the real question shifts:

**Option A — Engineer around the ceiling.** Accept Charles will always have these failure modes, and build the architecture to route around them:
- Tiny classifier on the front (regex / 1B model) intercepts simple greetings → returns "Charles here" without invoking the full agent
- Aggressively prune conversation context (5-turn window max, drop CC chatter when greeting arrives)
- Separate "social mode" vs "task mode" prompts — when user message is <20 chars or matches greeting patterns, switch to a 200-token social prompt
- Smaller, sharper system prompt overall (we did some of this today; could go further)

This is the realistic path inside your constraints. It doesn't make Charles smarter; it makes Charles avoid the situations where he's dumb.

**Option B — Test a dense ~13B model.** The A3B-4bit-MoE design might actually be losing to smaller DENSE models on simple-instruction-following tasks. Worth testing:
- Mistral Small 24B 4-bit (~12 GB) — dense, strong instruction-following
- Phi-4 14B 8-bit (~14 GB) — Microsoft's reasoning-focused dense model (not corporate-aligned at the model level, just from MS)
- Qwen2.5-Coder-32B 4-bit (~18 GB) — dense, was their earlier flagship before MoE; might be MORE coherent on basic stuff
- Llama-3.3-8B abliterated 8-bit (~9 GB) — small, uncensored, might surprise you on greetings

A dense 13B at 4-bit has more "active reasoning" per forward pass than A3B-3B-active and would leave plenty of room for Charles. The 7-min "hello" you saw on the other Qwen 32B was likely a different bottleneck (not enough quantization, or wrong serving config). A modern dense 13B-24B at 4-bit on M1 Ultra should be 2-5 sec for short replies.

**Option C — Hybrid.** Keep Charles 100% local for sovereignty-sensitive ops (memory, voice, watchdog, scheduled tasks). Route the conversational layer through a paid API (Claude Sonnet, DeepSeek API) for chat-quality. Compromises one principle to save another.

**Option D — Hardware upgrade.** Mac Studio M3 Ultra 96 GB or M4 Ultra 128 GB would unlock 70B at 4-bit comfortably. $5-10k. Real money. Probably the wrong call for now.

**My recommendation:** Option A + Option B in parallel. Option A is implementable in days (prompt + classifier + context pruning). Option B is a 1-hour download-and-test per candidate model — much cheaper than the framework rabbit hole. Try Mistral Small 24B 4-bit first; that's the closest dense match to Qwen's footprint and Mistral has historically been very good at instruction-following.

Don't migrate frameworks. The framework isn't your problem. The model's reasoning ceiling is, and a different model in the same size class might surprise you.

Why it's there: Qwen3 ships dual-mode. The model has a deliberation pathway (`<thinking>` trace, 500-3000 tokens of internal reasoning before the response) and a fast pathway. The Qwen team explicitly recommends disabling thinking for agentic/tool-use workloads — that's why it's off in Charles. The flag is there because Qwen built it to serve both research/math users (thinking on) AND agentic deployments (thinking off).

What it actually buys:

- **Math/logic/multi-step planning:** big jump in quality
- **Tool-call commitment** (the "narration-without-action" bug): probably helps, lets the model commit on a scratchpad before emitting
- **Hallucination** (fake URLs): mixed — thinking sometimes catches it, sometimes joins in
- **Conversational warmth:** zero help. Warmth lives in post-training register, not reasoning depth. Qwen will still sound Chinese-RLHF computer-y.

What it costs:
- Each Telegram reply gains 20-60 sec of pre-response thinking
- A 5-round tool chain becomes 2-5 minutes
- Your 15-second heartbeat tick won't fit — next tick fires while last is still thinking
- "Find that file" → 1.5 minutes total. Snail territory.

Found it — actual bug, not a state issue. **URL builder is mangling query strings.** Look at the server log:

```
GET /api/state/activity%3Flimit%3D100 HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
GET /api/state/conversations/8455750177%3Flimit%3D100 HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

The `?` and `=` are getting percent-encoded as part of the path. `appendingPathComponent` does that. So `?limit=100` becomes `%3Flimit%3D100` — the server sees no query string, and `/api/state/activity` literal returns 404 because the actual route includes the encoded garbage.

On Qwen — this is important and probably what's actually killing you:

**The "35B" is misleading.** Your model is `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`. The 35B is the TOTAL parameter count. The `A3B` means **Active 3 Billion** — it's a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model. Only 3B parameters do the actual work per token. The other 32B are sitting in expert slots that the router gates between based on the input.

What that means for tool calls specifically:

- For SPEED (token-throughput), it acts like a 3B model — super fast on your Mac Studio.
- For REASONING (judgment, holding many things in head at once), it acts like a 3B model — that's tiny. GPT-2 was 1.5B for reference.
- A dense 30B (no MoE) has 30B parameters working PER token. ~10× the active "thinking power" of your current model.

Why it nails the ideation step but fumbles the execution step: ideation only needs ONE coherent thought at a time (3B can manage that). Tool-call dispatch needs to hold simultaneously: (a) the user's goal, (b) the tool registry, (c) the correct schema for the chosen tool, (d) the args extracted from context, (e) the constraint of NOT claiming the call in text before emitting it. 5 things in head at once. 3B keeps dropping one.

The good news: your hardware can run a much heavier dense model. 64GB unified memory on M1 Ultra fits:

- **Qwen2.5-32B-Q4** (dense) — ~18GB RAM. Same disk footprint as your current MoE. ~10× more reasoning per token. ~5-8× slower throughput. THIS is the obvious A/B test.
- **Qwen2.5-72B-Q4** (dense) — ~38GB RAM. Probably handles tool calls almost as well as me. ~15× slower than current.

The Qwen2.5 series is dense (every param works every token). The Qwen3 series has the MoE you're running.
